CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is overall well-organized and presents its contributions (symmetric collaborative task, dataset, and DynoNet model) clearly early on, and the method section cleanly explains the dynamic knowledge graph embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction, and generation mechanisms. Notation is consistent and definitions are provided, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the model and experimental results (including human evaluations). Writing quality is good and free of major ambiguity, though the paper would benefit from an explicit discussion of limitations and clearer signposting of where hyperparameter and experimental details (e.g., seeds, code release) can be found to strengthen reproducibility. | B(4): Overall the paper is well-organized and the main contributions (symmetric collaborative task, 11K-dialogue dataset, and DynoNet model) are clearly stated early, matching the intro->method->experiments->conclusion flow. Notation appears consistent and the methodological description (dynamic graph Gt, message passing, entity abstraction, attention/copying) is concrete; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is described as good with thorough related work and hyperparameter details, though the absence of stated limitations suggests a minor presentation gap rather than a clarity failure. From an applied dialogue perspective the paper communicates its ideas clearly with only minor polish needed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is overall well-organized and presents its contributions (symmetric collaborative task, dataset, and DynoNet model) clearly early on, and the method section cleanly explains the dynamic knowledge graph embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction, and generation mechanisms. Notation is consistent and definitions are provided, and figures/tables/examples effectively illustrate the model and experimental results (including human evaluations). Writing quality is good and free of major ambiguity, though the paper would benefit from an explicit discussion of limitations and clearer signposting of where hyperparameter and experimental details (e.g., seeds, code release) can be found to strengthen reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well-organized and the main contributions (symmetric collaborative task, 11K-dialogue dataset, and DynoNet model) are clearly stated early, matching the intro->method->experiments->conclusion flow. Notation appears consistent and the methodological description (dynamic graph Gt, message passing, entity abstraction, attention/copying) is concrete; figures/tables and examples are reported as effective and informative. Writing quality is described as good with thorough related work and hyperparameter details, though the absence of stated limitations suggests a minor presentation gap rather than a clarity failure. From an applied dialogue perspective the paper communicates its ideas clearly with only minor polish needed.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper presents a notably novel assembly: a new symmetric collaborative dialogue formulation with a substantial 11K human-human dataset (MutualFriends) which, by itself, is a meaningful new problem/resource for the community. The proposed DynoNet—dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings with message passing, entity abstraction, and integrated attention/copying for generation—is a non-trivial, task-tailored combination that clearly differentiates from prior work (EntNet and simpler baselines) rather than being a mere rehash. That said, the core algorithmic components (message passing, copying, attention) are known primitives, and the paper does not claim new theoretical guarantees, so the contribution is notable but not fundamentally transformative. My judgment balances the new task/dataset and targeted model engineering against the lack of deeper theoretical innovation. | B(4): The paper presents a notably novel combination: a new symmetric collaborative dialogue task (MutualFriends) with a large 11K human-human dataset and a model (DynoNet) that tightly integrates dynamic knowledge-graph node embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction, attention and copying. Framing the task as symmetric collaboration with private KBs and collecting a large dataset is a meaningful new problem formulation for dialogue research, and the model architecture is a non-trivial extension beyond standard encoder–decoder and memory-net approaches (they explicitly compare to EntNet and rule baselines). The contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work and would likely inform experts working on structured KB integration for end-to-end dialogue, even if the innovations build on existing components.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a notably novel assembly: a new symmetric collaborative dialogue formulation with a substantial 11K human-human dataset (MutualFriends) which, by itself, is a meaningful new problem/resource for the community. The proposed DynoNet—dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings with message passing, entity abstraction, and integrated attention/copying for generation—is a non-trivial, task-tailored combination that clearly differentiates from prior work (EntNet and simpler baselines) rather than being a mere rehash. That said, the core algorithmic components (message passing, copying, attention) are known primitives, and the paper does not claim new theoretical guarantees, so the contribution is notable but not fundamentally transformative. My judgment balances the new task/dataset and targeted model engineering against the lack of deeper theoretical innovation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents a notably novel combination: a new symmetric collaborative dialogue task (MutualFriends) with a large 11K human-human dataset and a model (DynoNet) that tightly integrates dynamic knowledge-graph node embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction, attention and copying. Framing the task as symmetric collaboration with private KBs and collecting a large dataset is a meaningful new problem formulation for dialogue research, and the model architecture is a non-trivial extension beyond standard encoder–decoder and memory-net approaches (they explicitly compare to EntNet and rule baselines). The contributions are clearly differentiated from prior work and would likely inform experts working on structured KB integration for end-to-end dialogue, even if the innovations build on existing components.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear claims, introduces a novel DynoNet model, and provides substantial empirical evidence on a large human-human dataset showing improvements over reasonable baselines. The main weaknesses are missing ablation/statistical-significance analyses and lack of explicit reproducibility details (code/data release), which temper but do not invalidate the technical contributions. Given the convincing empirical results but these notable evaluation gaps, a slightly above-medium score is warranted.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents clear claims and extensive experiments on a sizable 11K-dialogue dataset and proposes a concrete DynoNet architecture; empirical comparisons to rule-based and neural baselines (including EntNet) support task effectiveness. However, from the summary there is no formal problem definition or theoretical analysis/guarantees, ablation details and statistical significance tests are not reported, and code/data release is not mentioned — all of which my reviewer persona requires for strong technical validation. These omissions raise concerns about reproducibility and attribution of gains to specific model components, so while the results are promising they lack the rigorous analysis I demand.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper clearly states the main claims and provides substantial empirical evidence: a large 11K human-human dataset, a novel DynoNet architecture, and automatic plus human evaluations showing improved task success and human-likeness compared to rule and neural baselines (including discussion of EntNet). There appear to be no formal mathematical derivations to check, and the experimental setup uses appropriate baselines and human-partner metrics; however, the summary does not report statistical significance tests or discuss potential confounds (e.g., ablations or robustness to KB variations), which are minor gaps. Overall the experiments and analyses are convincing for the core technical claims, but a few missing evaluation details prevent a perfect score.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses a topical problem and makes substantial empirical contributions (large human-human dataset and a novel DynoNet architecture) that suit a top NLP/ML venue. Reviewer A’s concerns about limited baselines, lack of formalization/theoretical analysis, and unclear reproducibility temper enthusiasm, suggesting it is strong but not flawless; given the solid dataset, model, and human evaluation reported by Reviewer B, a slightly above-midpoint score of 4 reflects the paper’s clear relevance and empirical value while acknowledging the methodological gaps to address.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses a topical NLP/ML problem (symmetric collaborative dialogue with KBs) and proposes a novel neural architecture (DynoNet) with a large human-human dataset, so it targets the right community. However, from the summary it appears to lack a formal problem definition or theoretical analysis and does not report code release or clear oracle/upper-bound comparisons, and it compares to only a small set of baselines (rule, a few neural models, EntNet) rather than three strong SOTA baselines and thorough ablations with statistical tests. Given these gaps in formalization, reproducibility guarantees, and baseline breadth, the paper is borderline appropriate for a top venue.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is an excellent fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it introduces a well-motivated symmetric collaborative dialogue task and contributes a large human-human dataset (11K dialogues), which aligns with my emphasis on substantial datasets and clear collection. The proposed DynoNet model integrates structured KB (dynamic graph embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction) with end-to-end generation and is evaluated with appropriate automatic metrics and thorough human partner evaluations (fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness). The methodology, baselines (rule, neural, EntNet comparison), and thorough related work make it directly relevant to the task-oriented dialogue and representation learning communities. Overall the empirical contribution (dataset + model + human evaluation) targets the right audience and standards for a top-tier conference.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides broadly appropriate and fair comparisons using the same dataset, several neural and rule-based baselines, and both automatic and human evaluations. Reviewer A’s concerns about a few missing stronger baselines, explicit oracle/upper-bound comparisons, and limited reproducibility (code not reported) are reasonable and prevent a full top score, so I assign a 4 to reflect solid empirical comparison with room for greater rigor and transparency.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against a rule-based system, several baseline neural models and a known architecture (EntNet), and uses consistent data and human-evaluation metrics on their MutualFriends dataset, so comparisons are broadly appropriate and fair. However, from the summary it is unclear that there are three strong, state-of-the-art neural baselines or explicit oracle/upper-bound comparisons, and the write-up omits mention of acknowledging when prior methods match or outperform theirs; code release is also not reported, limiting reproducibility of the comparisons. Overall the empirical comparison is solid but missing a couple of rigorous baselines and transparency guarantees I would expect.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates against a strong set of baselines including rule-based, baseline neural models, and a relevant comparative architecture (EntNet), and uses the same MutualFriends dataset and shared human-partner evaluation metrics (fluency, correctness, cooperation, human-likeness) for comparisons. Comparisons appear fair and reproducible (hyperparameters reported), and the paper discusses related work thoroughly; results include both automatic and human evaluations so prior methods’ relative performance is acknowledged. From a practical dialogue-research perspective this constitutes comprehensive and fair comparison with relevant baselines.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important, underexplored problem and provides a substantial 11K human-human dataset plus a novel DynoNet model that meaningfully integrates dynamic KGs with generation—contributions likely to spur follow-on work. Reviewer A’s concerns about limited formal analysis, ablations, baselines, and reproducibility temper claims of transformational impact, so while not field-changing, the work has clear strong impact and utility for the community, meriting a score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a relevant and practical problem—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs—and contributes a sizable 11K human-human dataset plus a novel DynoNet architecture that jointly models dynamic KGs and utterance generation, which should be useful to the community. However, from the provided summary the work lacks formalization and theoretical analysis of the task/model, and it is unclear whether sufficiently strong ablation studies, statistical testing, and a broad set of state-of-the-art neural baselines (≥3) or code release are provided to ensure reproducibility and convince skeptical researchers. Given these strengths (dataset, promising modeling idea) and these limitations, the impact is moderate: likely to be built upon but not yet field-changing.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important and underexplored problem—symmetric collaborative dialogue with private KBs—and supplies a large 11K human-human dataset, which alone is a valuable resource for the community. The DynoNet model meaningfully integrates dynamic knowledge-graph embeddings with end-to-end generation (message passing, entity abstraction, attention/copy), and human evaluations show improved task success and human-likeness over rule and neural baselines. These contributions are likely to change how researchers combine structured KBs with conversational generation and to spur follow-on work and dataset-driven studies, though the work stops short of being clearly transformative across NLP as a whole.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a strong empirical contribution with a large dataset, a novel DynoNet architecture, and thorough comparisons that support the main claims. However, both also correctly note missing ablation/sensitivity analyses, discussion of failure modes/limitations, and limited reproducibility details, which prevent a higher rating. Given the solid empirical work but these meaningful omissions, a score of 4 fairly reflects the paper’s strengths and the gaps that should be addressed.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper provides a substantial dataset (11K dialogues), a novel DynoNet architecture, and both automatic and human evaluations comparing to a rule-based system and neural baselines (including EntNet), which supports the main empirical claims to a degree. However, from the summary there is no formal problem formulation or theoretical analysis (penalized by my theory-focused standards), ablation/sensitivity analysis is not clearly reported, limitations and failure modes are not discussed, and code/data release is not mentioned despite only hyperparameters being reported. These gaps leave important reproducibility and attribution questions unanswered, so the experimental work is adequate but missing key analyses required for stronger confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents strong empirical work: a large human-human dataset (11K dialogues), a model that tightly integrates dynamic KG embeddings with generation, and both automatic and human evaluations showing improved task success and human-likeness. Related work coverage and experimental comparisons (rule, neural baselines, EntNet) appear thorough and hyperparameters are reported, supporting reproducibility. However, the summary does not mention explicit ablation or sensitivity analyses of key components (e.g., message passing or entity abstraction), and the paper does not discuss failure cases or limitations — these are important gaps for fully supporting the claims. Overall the experiments are solid with minor but meaningful omissions.

main_claims: Introduce a symmetric collaborative dialogue task where two agents with private knowledge bases must communicate to find a shared item.
Collected a large human-human dataset of 11K dialogues exhibiting diverse lexical, semantic, and strategic phenomena.

Propose a neural model (DynoNet) using dynamic knowledge graph embeddings, message passing, entity abstraction, attention and copying to jointly model structured KBs and unstructured dialogue and to generate utterances.
Automatic and human evaluations show the proposed model is more effective at achieving the task goal and more human-like than rule-based and baseline neural models.
_summary.methodology: An RNN-based encoder-decoder architecture that maintains a dynamic knowledge graph Gt (nodes for items, attributes, entities) whose node embeddings are updated via message passing; uses entity abstraction so entities are represented by structural/contextual embeddings; generation uses attention over node embeddings and a copying mechanism to produce tokens or entity realizations.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - task-oriented / collaborative dialogue with knowledge graph embeddings

reviewer_a.name: Theory-focused Skeptic
reviewer_a.background: Senior researcher in machine learning and theoretical NLP with a PhD in computer science; experience in formalizing dialogue systems and analyzing model generalization.
traits: Penalizes empirical-only papers that lack formal problem definition or theoretical guarantees (deducts points if no formalization or analysis of task/model is provided).
Requires strong ablation studies and statistical significance tests (penalizes if key components are not individually evaluated or significance not reported).
Penalizes papers with fewer than three strong, state-of-the-art neural baselines or without clear oracle/upper-bound comparisons.
Reduces score if code, data schemas, and training details (hyperparameters, seeds) are not released or are insufficient for reproducibility.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Prioritizes theoretical clarity and rigorous empirical attribution: accepts empirical work only if accompanied by formalization, thorough ablations, and reproducibility guarantees.

reviewer_b.name: Applied Dialogue Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Experienced NLP researcher and practitioner focused on task-oriented and collaborative dialogue systems; has led dataset collection and human evaluation studies for deployed conversational agents.
traits: Rewards novel, well-executed task formulations and large human-human datasets (gives bonus for datasets >5K dialogues and clear annotation/collection protocol).
Favors models that integrate structured knowledge with end-to-end generation and demonstrate improved task success in automatic and human evaluations (downgrades less for lack of formal analysis).
Values thorough human evaluation and qualitative analyses of dialog strategies and linguistic phenomena (requires human studies and error analysis to be present).
Is lenient on theoretical guarantees or formal proofs if the empirical contribution (dataset + model + human eval) is substantial and clearly documented.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Emphasizes practical impact: prefers papers that introduce useful tasks/datasets and demonstrate empirically effective, well-evaluated models, even with limited formal analysis.
